result:
The first thing I notice when I step outside at 5:47 a.m. is how quiet the world still pretends to be. Streetlights hum their lonely orange song while the sky loosens its grip on darkness, one reluctant shade at a time. A single crow rattles the silence like it’s personally offended by the peace. I like these stolen hours before the city remembers its own name.

I’ve been thinking lately about how everything alive is basically a temporary agreement between chaos and pattern. Trees don’t panic about growing leaves. They just do it, like breathing. Somewhere between our first conscious thought and our last unnecessary worry, we forget that we, too, are mostly made of the same stubborn insistence on becoming that a dandelion has when it cracks through concrete.

There’s a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that I keep returning to: the beauty of things that are imperfect, impermanent, and incomplete. The chip in your favorite mug. The way your grandmother’s handwriting got shakier every year but somehow more honest. The fact that every person you love will one day be a story you tell someone younger who never got to meet them. These aren’t tragedies to solve. They’re the texture of being real.

I don’t have grand conclusions this morning. Just the soft satisfaction of drinking coffee while it’s still too hot, watching the sky turn the color of peach flesh, and remembering that for now, at least, I get to be one of the lucky ones who gets to witness all of this beautiful, ridiculous, heartbreaking aliveness.

(Word count: 248)
